sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A demography class examined how age structure affects dependency ratios. In one region, children under 18 made up 22% of the population, adults 18–64 made up 60%, and adults 65 and older made up 18%. In a second region, the corresponding percentages were 19%, 55%, and 26%. Researchers also compared the ratio of working-age adults to older adults: 3.3:1 in the first region and 2.1:1 in the second. The second region spent a larger share of its public budget on elder-care services.
questions.stem: Which region has the larger proportion of adults age 65 and older?

choices.D: The passage does not provide this information

explanations.C: Correct. Region 2 has 26% of its population age 65 and older, compared with 18% in Region 1. The figure and passage both show Region 2 as the older population.
explanations.A: Region 1 has the smaller older-adult share.
explanations.B: The percentages are clearly not equal.
explanations.D: The necessary information is stated directly in the passage and figure.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Population age distribution in two regions with different dependency patterns.

questions.stem: The lower working-age-to-older-adult ratio in Region 2 most directly suggests what?

choices.A: There are fewer working-age adults available per older adult to support age-related services
choices.B: Region 2 has no children in the population
choices.C: Older adults in Region 2 are biologically healthier by definition
choices.D: Public budgets cannot be compared across regions

explanations.A: Correct. A smaller support ratio means each older adult is backed by fewer people in the working-age population. That can create greater fiscal and caregiving pressure, which fits the larger elder-care budget share reported for Region 2.
explanations.B: Region 2 still has children in the population, just a slightly smaller share.
explanations.C: The age ratio does not by itself tell us that older adults are healthier.
explanations.D: The passage directly compares public-budget emphasis between the two regions.

questions.stem: Why would Region 2 likely devote more public resources to elder care?

choices.D: Its larger older population creates greater demand for age-related services
choices.A: Because its child population is necessarily larger
choices.B: Because dependency ratios are unrelated to policy
choices.C: Because fewer older adults require more elder-care spending

explanations.D: Correct. The second region has both a larger proportion of older adults and a lower worker-to-older-adult support ratio. Those conditions are consistent with higher demand for healthcare, transport, and long-term support services.
explanations.A: Region 2 actually has a slightly smaller child share than Region 1.
explanations.B: The passage specifically suggests that age structure matters for public spending.
explanations.C: The spending pattern is tied to a larger, not smaller, older population.

questions.stem: A policymaker responding to Region 2’s age structure might most reasonably prioritize which policy?

choices.B: Expanding services that support aging populations and caregivers
choices.C: Assuming school enrollment will rise because the 65+ group is larger
choices.D: Ignoring dependency patterns because they affect only households, not public systems

explanations.B: Correct. Given the larger older-adult share and smaller support ratio, policies that strengthen elder care, transportation, and caregiver support would match the demographic pressures shown in the passage.
explanations.A: The passage suggests demand is higher, not lower, for elder-care programs.
explanations.C: A larger 65+ group does not imply rising school enrollment.
explanations.D: Dependency patterns influence both households and public institutions.

questions.stem: Which statement is best supported by the evidence provided?

choices.C: Regions can differ in age structure in ways that plausibly influence how public resources are allocated
choices.A: Age structure determines every policy choice with certainty
choices.B: The older population share proves one region is wealthier
choices.D: Dependency ratios are irrelevant if children make up less than one quarter of the population

explanations.C: Correct. The data show clear demographic differences and a related difference in budget emphasis. That supports a plausible connection between age structure and policy priorities without justifying deterministic or unsupported claims about wealth.
explanations.A: Policy is influenced by many factors, so age structure does not determine every choice with certainty.
explanations.B: Wealth is not established by the age percentages alone.
explanations.D: The child share does not make dependency ratios irrelevant; older-adult dependency remains important.

questions.passage: A sociologist interviewed multigenerational households in two neighborhoods. In Neighborhood A, 48% of households reported that a grandparent provided weekly childcare, compared with 27% in Neighborhood B. Adults in Neighborhood A also reported lower average out-of-pocket childcare costs and more frequent shared meals among extended kin. However, some interviewees in both neighborhoods described tension when caregiving expectations were unevenly distributed. The study used interviews and household surveys rather than experimental assignment.
questions.stem: Which neighborhood reported more weekly grandparent childcare involvement?

choices.B: Both neighborhoods reported the same involvement
choices.C: The passage does not indicate this

explanations.D: Correct. Neighborhood A reported weekly grandparent childcare in 48% of households, compared with 27% in Neighborhood B. That makes Neighborhood A the neighborhood with greater kin-based childcare involvement.
explanations.A: Neighborhood B had the lower reported rate.
explanations.B: The percentages are different, not equal.
explanations.C: The relevant information is stated directly in the passage.

questions.stem: Lower out-of-pocket childcare costs in Neighborhood A most directly suggest what sociological concept?

choices.A: Family networks can provide instrumental support that substitutes for market services

explanations.A: Correct. When grandparents provide childcare, families may spend less on paid services. That is a classic example of instrumental support embedded in kinship networks.
explanations.B: The looking-glass self concerns self-concept, not childcare support.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation is a perceptual concept unrelated to family networks.
explanations.D: Clinical depression is not the concept illustrated by reduced childcare costs.

questions.stem: Why is it important that some interviewees reported tension about caregiving expectations?

choices.B: Supportive family arrangements can also create role strain and conflict
choices.A: The tension proves kin support is never beneficial
choices.C: The interviews therefore invalidate the childcare-cost findings
choices.D: Tension means the households were not really multigenerational

explanations.B: Correct. The passage shows that kin support brings both benefits and potential burdens. Uneven expectations can create strain, which is an important nuance in understanding how family systems operate.
explanations.A: The passage does not say support was never beneficial; it describes both benefits and tensions.
explanations.C: Conflict does not invalidate the observed cost and childcare patterns.
explanations.D: Households can still be multigenerational even if conflict exists.

questions.stem: A city program trying to support these families would most likely help by doing what?

choices.C: Providing caregiver resources while acknowledging both the benefits and burdens of kin support
choices.A: Discouraging all grandparent involvement in childcare
choices.B: Ignoring informal caregivers because only paid care matters
choices.D: Assuming all family members experience caregiving in the same way

explanations.C: Correct. A useful program would recognize that family care can lower costs and strengthen support while also producing strain. Resources such as respite services or caregiver education would respond to both sides of the pattern.
explanations.A: The passage shows that grandparent care can be helpful, so discouraging it entirely is not justified.
explanations.B: Informal caregiving clearly matters in the households studied.
explanations.D: The interviews show that experiences vary and are not identical across family members.

questions.stem: What is the clearest methodological limitation of the study?

choices.A: It describes associations in observed households and cannot prove that multigenerational living caused the differences
choices.B: It used both interviews and surveys
choices.C: It included two neighborhoods instead of one
choices.D: It discussed weekly childcare instead of daily childcare

explanations.A: Correct. Because households were not experimentally assigned to different family structures, other neighborhood or economic differences could contribute to the observed patterns. The design is informative but observational.
explanations.B: Using more than one method can strengthen understanding rather than create the main limitation.
explanations.C: Comparing two neighborhoods can be useful and is not itself the major inferential problem.
explanations.D: Weekly versus daily childcare is a detail, not the core limitation in causal inference.
